original_abstract: Non-targeted liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) is a widely used tool for metabolomics analysis, enabling the detection and annotation of small molecules in complex environmental samples. Data-dependent acquisition (DDA) of product ion spectra is thereby currently one of the most frequently applied data acquisition strategies. The optimization of DDA parameters is central to ensuring high spectral quality, coverage, and number of compound annotations. Here, we evaluated the influence of 10 central DDA settings of the Q Exactive mass spectrometer on natural organic matter samples from ocean, river, and soil environments. After data analysis with classical and feature-based molecular networking using MZmine and GNPS, we compared the total number of network nodes, multivariate clustering, and spectrum quality-related metrics such as annotation and singleton rates, MS/MS placement, and coverage. Our results show that automatic gain control, microscans, mass resolving power, and dynamic exclusion are the most critical parameters, whereas collision energy, TopN, and isolation width had moderate and apex trigger, monoisotopic selection, and isotopic exclusion minor effects. The insights into the data acquisition ergonomics of the Q Exactive platform presented here can guide new users and provide them with initial method parameters, some of which may also be transferable to other sample types and MS platforms.
humanized_abstract: Current non-targeted liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) approaches in metabolomics rely heavily on the use of high-sensitivity detectors and employ a suite of experimental approaches to elucidate the composition of small molecules present in environmental samples. Of these, the routine application of data-dependent acquisition (DDA) of product ion spectra is almost ubiquitous. A proper selection of parameters in DDA is necessary to achieve the optimal performance in terms of mass spectrum resolution and maximum description of the analytes present in the sample. Here, we investigated how different parameters affect the performance of Q Exactive DDA (10 parameters tested) when analysing representative natural organic matter derived from different marine, fresh-water and soil environments. We used classical data analysis workflows in conjunction with data processed with MZmine using a feature-based approach for molecular networking via GNPS and then evaluated network properties in terms of number of nodes, clustering patterns and spectrum features (annotation/singleton rates, %MS/MS placed and %spectrum coverage). Overall, parameters like automatic gain control, microscans, mass resolving power and dynamic exclusion showed the most significant impact, while others, such as collision energy, TopN and isolation width exhibited moderate effects on the characteristics of the mass spectra. By contrast, other parameters such as the apex trigger, monoisotopic selection and isotopic exclusion showed minor effects on mass spectra. Basic optimization suggestions of the parameters based on the findings are given for beginners in the use of the Q Exactive, together with a first guess for possible parameter optimisation on other types of samples or MS equipment.
